title: The $60 billion resource hiding in space, and the startup trying to mine it | E2268

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: All right, everybody, welcome back to This Week in Startups. It's Friday, that means Lon Harris is here with me. And at the end of the show, we will do Off Duty with Lon. Everybody loves that segment where Lon takes us off duty and we share what we're buying, what we're consuming, what we're watching.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Let me just start the show by saying, I love Plaud. Um, I'm using my Plaud pin right now to record this very conversation.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: Mine too.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Oh you have yours too? Did you activate yet?

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: Oh yeah, no, it's running. It's been - my Plaud is - my Plaud goes all day.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yes, and I have mine running now and I just, you know, it's got the red light so I'm being privacy first. And you know, I only use it when people know I'm using it. But this is the interesting thing. You could wear the Plaud pin like you're wearing it, or you can wear it on your wrist. Another great way to wear it. And what I do with this is, when I'm hiking around the ranch or I have a moment - Plaud, take a memo, action item: we need to move two of our researchers into the paid newsletter that we're doing. Please have Lon pick his number one and two, even though that's super uncomfortable to ask in front of everybody, the one and two. Which is why you didn't answer it.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: I sent you my one and two picks privately. I don't like to pick favorites in front of people.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: See? No. I like to create a little bit of like, who's ready for this? Anyway, that's my management style. But Plaud is amazing. And what it does is, it creates a summary and then you can share that summary. Then they have like 20 different - I don't know, maybe it's hundreds, but I have like 20 really nice templates I use, like full accurate transcript, it learns who Lon is in my life, it learns who Alex is, it knows them, so then for all time forward I don't have to mention that. It creates a mind map of the conversation we have when we have a strategy meeting. All this great stuff, and then one-click share. Why is it better than using your phone? Oh my god, your phone's microphone stinks. And you have to though clumsily go through your phone as opposed to beep, one button, you're ready to go.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: Yeah, it's the contextual - it understands the conversation and really thinks about how to organize it for you. That's the really...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: What's the offer? What's the offer? We have an offer?

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: We do have an offer, Jason, of course. So you can check it out at plaud.ai/twist and if you use the code twist, they're going to give you 10% off your new Plaud pin. That's P-L-A-U-D .ai /twist.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: And please, if you do have a good experience with it, share it on social and tell everybody you found out about it on This Week in Startups, on TWiST. We'd really appreciate it. Please.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: All right, let's get started. We did our first thank you to our sponsors. But we've got a really great first guest. I'm excited about this one.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: I'm - I'm excited. I saw - I saw some of their tweets going by, I read a little bit about the startup and I immediately pinged the team like, let's - let's get these guys on. I want - I want to learn more about this. So we've got Matt Gialich, he's the CEO and co-founder of AstroForge. They are a mining startup, they're developing spacecraft to go prospect and extract platinum group metals or PGMs from near-Earth asteroids.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Jason, that's the plan.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: So literally science fiction is reality. Matt, welcome—

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Matt, welcome back. Have you been on the program before, Matt, or is this your first time?

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: I talked to you maybe three years ago when we first—

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: I remember. Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: —we first launched, before we had ever launched our first spacecraft.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yeah. So congratulations. Where are you at with mining asteroids?

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Look, this is a really hard thing to do. I mean, we are the first commercial company that really wants to go outside of the Earth-moon system and explore the solar system. Right? It's a totally big swing on space. We've seen a lot of venture dollars go into space, a lot of companies start up into space, and we're kind of that next step in what you can go do. Right now we're building Deep Space 2, it's our next spacecraft that's a 200-kilogram spacecraft that'll launch later this year on a Falcon 9 that goes to the moon and it'll attempt to go out and potentially land on an asteroid.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: Wow, I want to jump in here with- I- I was doing a little research on this. When you say land on an asteroid, are we talking about like the rocket like ship comes down like in the sci-fi and it lands on the surface? It's more is there it's sort of like a docking procedure like with the International Space Station, correct?

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: It's much more like a docking procedure. I mean, we have to kind of like, what the movies tell you and what's actually out there are two very different things. Right? We go to what are called near-Earth asteroids, they're pretty close to the planet. The one we're targeting right now is about 10 million miles away. And these asteroids are around 200 meters in diameter. So in the terms of space, these are very, very small things in space that we want to go land on. Now, we're also looking for a very specific type of asteroid. We're looking for an asteroid called a metal asteroid. And because of that, these asteroids are about 70% iron, somewhere in that range, iron-nickel, which means they're magnetic. So the way we land on them is simply with magnets. We stick to the asteroid.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: Because Armageddon, which is my big, you know, cultural point for landing on an asteroid, it's like they're crashing into the surface, people are shimmied back and forth, but it's really, it's more delicate than that?

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: No Steve Buscemi in this scenario. These are unmanned, you land, you take a sample, and then eventually, Matt, am I correct that you want to build like a more semi-permanent installation on one of those when it's in orbit or like what is the window for you to actually drill into an asteroid consistently and then get it back to Earth? Right? Because that's the other piece you want to get these back to Earth or do you want to use it for facilitating manufacturing in space?

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: The problem right now is on the planet. The problem right now is not in space. While we'd love to supply the in-space economy, currently right now there isn't an in-space economy, right? I'm a firm believer that there is still a mass problem to space. Now Elon has done tremendous things to lower the price of mass to get into the solar system. That all being said, you're still charged by the kilogram. That is still the underlying thing you pay for. So building a small craft that can go do this is where we see the sweet spot to be. We also think about this at volume, Jason. So it's not about can you build one huge ship that goes and does this, it's—

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Can you make hundreds, if not thousands of them that are going to go out to these asteroids, mine them and bring it back to Earth? But look guys, we got one planet. We got one habitable planet in the solar system as of today. We gotta protect it. And mining is one of the most destructive things we do on this planet. There's no other way to have our way of life unless we go out in the cosmos and mine it for its resources.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Walk us through this video we're seeing here.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: I think one thing that is always surprising if we talk about asteroids everybody says like 'oh that's sci-fi, you can't mine an asteroid.' This is literally a NASA mission that went out and mined an asteroid. This is Bennu, it's run by a guy named Dante Lauretta, worked on it for 22 years. Um he's an advisor to us and this one went out to a different type of asteroid than we want to go to and took a sample of it and brought it back to Earth. This sample returned about I think about a year and a half ago. A couple members of the team are on our staff here. Um and they got a whole bunch of information about this asteroid. What's super important about that mission to us though is it updated the models for how we look at asteroids. So it helped us understand the types that are in the solar system.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: And uh what are we seeing here? What is that machine or satellite? Is that your actual miner that we see there?

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: What you're seeing here is maybe look, I was in elementary school in the 90s. I don't know about all the listeners right, but uh in the 90s we heard about this asteroid belt and it's where all the asteroids were. Nobody had ever discovered any asteroids close to the Earth. In fact, at the turn of the century there was about seven asteroids that were classified as near-earth asteroids. As of today we have over 600,000 of them cataloged. So what I'm actually showing you here is that what you were told 20 years ago is very different. There's a lot of objects really close to our home that we can go mine. Um and that makes this a much shorter mission and I think a much more viable mission to go do.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: All right. Amazing. And so um what does the actual miner look like? Do we have the actual miner and what it looks like and what it actually does? Like explain that to me.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: There is a picture on Twitter that we just put up. Yeah, here it is of of one of the first phases of mining. Now mining is one of the biggest IP things we have at the company, but I'll tell you guys in short how this works. It's a pretty simple way to think about it. We use directed energy, i.e. lasers, to remove material from the asteroid and then we sort it out with magnetism. Platinum group metals are not magnetic, iron-nickel are, and it's a very simple way to think about sorting out the material to bring it back. The spacecraft we have is designed to bring back up to 1,000 kilograms of material, which in today's dollars... now this stuff, look it's a commodity, it fluctuates all the time. As of today it's worth about 105 million dollars per mission. So that's kind of the upper cost target we gotta be below to make this work. And the whole point of the company J-Cal is how do we turn that little knob of risk? Right? How far can we turn that knob of risk to lower the cost to get the cheapest spacecraft in space to go mine that asteroid and go out. Now Deep Space 2, in total, including the launch, is going to cost about 10.4 million dollars. That's a very different price point than anybody's ever tried to go out into the solar system with before.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: So 10 million investment per mission with a potential return of a hundy. That's that's a lot. Now if you just hit it, you know being an angel investor and doing this for a living or...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: A poker player. Hey, you just need to have this happen one out of every ten times in order for you to break even. So, does- is that mean it's a sure thing at this point? Do you feel confident it's a sure thing that you'll be able to get one in ten back?

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: I want to tell you about a product I'm obsessed with and everyone in tech seems to be obsessed with. It's called WhisperFlow. W-I-S-P-R Flow. You set it up and then you hit a key combination and you talk, and it transcribes what you say. It does it more accurately and quicker and across all your platforms than any other speech-to-text software I've ever used. When you communicate all day long like I do, even as a fast typist, it is going to be three, four, five times faster than you can type. And because it fixes all of your ums and your ahs and your mistakes and cleans it up, all of the punctuation, all of the filler and unnecessary words being removed in real time. WhisperFlow is already available everywhere. When I'm using it in Superhuman Gmail, Slack, Google Docs, I use it everywhere. The messages sound like I wrote them. We're moving into a world where agents are going to make us a hundred times more efficient. Now, you add WhisperFlow making you five, ten, twenty times more efficient. Visit whisperflow.ai/twist to get started for free today. This product's amazing. That's W-I-S-P-R-F-L-O-W.ai/twist. I've been using this product for six months. It is extraordinary.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: I'd be lying if I said I was 100% confident we could do this. I'm a founder with a thesis that we can get out to the universe for $10 million and go mine an asteroid. And as soon as I'm 100% confident, that means I'm not paying attention to all the things that can go wrong. Like, this is a very technical company and a very technical job as CEO to go run and figure out if we can do this. And it's kind of what we spend all our time doing, JCal. I mean, like, it is really from- I think Elon showed us the way here of: be one of the best engineers on the team that can go develop these products. You have to know this in and out of how we're going to go do this. And that's what I spend all my time doing.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Remind us again, when does this mining mission occur? And are we going to get to watch it live? I mean, one of the things I've been amazed over the years is that Elon has really high fidelity video from all of these, you know, different missions now. Like even the multiple stages. I'm not sure how he does that, I'm assuming Starlink is involved. I know they have like helicopters and drones, you know, doing the low earth- or doing the atmosphere video. But take us into bringing the world into watching you do this. Is that a key part of this and is that a key part of, I think, Elon's success with SpaceX is bringing humanity along for the ride through live streaming?

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: I'm going to be honest with you, I wish we could live stream at the asteroid. The answer is we cannot. Uh, when we're at the asteroid and the way we save money is very simple: we lower our data rate dramatically. So at the asteroid, the max we're talking to spacecraft at is about 400 bits per second. That is not a video stream quality at all, right? This is like barely being-

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: able to talk to the spacecraft. I mean, that's how you keep it cheap. What we do do, and what we did do with Odin, our last mission we launched, right, is we are full transparency. We livestream the entire control room, all of the data we see, anybody else can see. I mean, I believe in transparency up and down. In fact, when Odin failed, we wrote a ton of articles that are on our website. You can go read about it, exactly what happened and exactly where we got it wrong and how we plan on fixing it because at the end of the day, that's how you learn and get better at this and—

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: So what, take us through that. The mission two was Odin, and mission three is Vesta. When did— and that Odin happened March 2024, right?

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Odin was February 27th, 2025, right? Last year. So about— about 11 months ago was when we launched Odin. Odin was our first attempt to go out into deep space. It launched on IM-2. So, this is a rocket launch going to trans-lunar injection, right? This goes to the moon. We leave from the Falcon and we slingshot around the moon out to the asteroid. What happened on Odin— to be clear, Odin was a total pathfinder for us. It was the first commercial deep space mission ever attempted. We got the first license from the FCC to ever go into deep space as a purely commercial company with no government interest. Um, and we had a failure right away. One of the failures that happened was the solar panels did not deploy. So the spacecraft's in a very slow spin where we were not power positive. We were still able to talk to it. We were still able to communicate with it. In fact, we were still able to prove out a lot of the technical navigation philosophies we used on that mission. But at the end of the day, like, we didn't get to the asteroid, right? We didn't do good enough. We gotta do better on the next one.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: I did think one thing that was interesting, so far, the— the two missions, neither of which went right so far, they were both like communication failures. It wasn't like the— the craft exploded or spun out into the galaxy. It was like you kind of lost touch with it at some point.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: I think I do a shitty job explaining this. I try to explain it this way, like, the way you die as a human is your brain stops sending electrical signals. The cause of your death is cancer, heart attack, whatever— car accident, whatever that is, right? So like, communication will always be what it seems like. Odin communicated perfectly. We didn't have a communication problem on Odin. What we had on Odin was a power issue. So, we weren't able to turn on the radios. That was why it manifested as a communication issue. So, again, we proved out a lot on that mission, but we gotta do better on deep space too. We gotta make it all the way.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: And as a startup, how many swings at bat do you get? And— and talk to me about the investors in the company. I think you've raised like 50 million bucks. How do they look at this? You know, and how do you communicate? Hey, this is a— you know, pretty binary thing. Either it works or it doesn't. And I know Elon struggled with this in the early days of SpaceX, so take me through that struggle and communication with investors.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: I mean, it's hard. You need to have special people that are willing to jump aboard this ship and see the future with you, right, and really can take a— a deep look at the future. Now, I want to give a lot of credit to Elon for paving the way and showing investors that it's possible to have these outsized returns if you're willing to take outsized risk, right? Like, this is a hard thing to go pull off. We do have some amazing investors. I mean, Initialized Capital, Gary Tan is who led our seed round, right? And then we went through Y Combinator. 776 is—

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: big investor in us, we have some massive angels that have really helped us get over the line. And uh, being super transparent with the investors about what we're doing and the risk we're taking, I think is one of the things that we're really good at. Like our investor monthly letters are giant documents that I write as a way to not only document what we're doing at the company but to show people the progress we're making. It is all about incremental progress as you go here. I mean guys, Elon failed three times with Falcon 1 before he got there with what, the fourth launch. This, we're going even farther and we're going after something even bigger. It's gonna be a rough road and you gotta kind of strap in and expect it.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Now, there are some materials that exist on asteroids that don't exist on Earth or they're very rare on Earth, am I correct?

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: Not really J-Cal, I'm gonna be very honest with you about this. The material we're going after is platinum group metals, right? So the six elements that make up platinum, iridium, what, you can go look them up and see what they all are. We don't have any of them in the continental United States. So not only is there a huge geological play here, and you've seen China especially with the rare earths really flex their muscle here and ban us from having gallium and some of these other materials. I mean Trump put out an huge executive order on minerals, right? We need mineral sovereignty as the US. There is no way to like make this up or go get it, the minerals that we have discovered are way too deep in the ground for us to go mine. The most economical play though is to go to space. And I think that's the big difference. This isn't necessarily a security of resources, I think this is a better economical advantage than opening a new platinum mine in South Africa would be.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Yeah, and these PGMs, that's the key. Those, palladium, rhodium, osmium...

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: Osmium, ruthenium and iridium I believe.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I might have been a little tired in my chemistry class 40 years ago, but but tell us about what they're used for here on Earth and if you could at scale, you know, bring down... because I understand like one asteroid could have more than we have on Earth, and I don't know if that's true or not, but that was some of my cursory research when I first met...

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: It's yeah, I mean look, there's a lot of this stuff in space that we can go after. What is it used for? Every single thing that every VC invests in. How do we make computer chips? You make it with iridium, with iridium crucibles to melt silicon. Like, this is used in every walk of your life. If you're looking at an LCD screen right now or OLED screen, they have three of the six platinum group metals in it. We touch it every day across the board. It's in cancer therapies, it's also in how we limit radi- emissions on your car. This stuff is just used worldwide. That's why it's a commodity, right? It's not like some special thing we're making up. It is a commodity metal that we want to go provide. Now you ask the question that I can't answer, which is, hey, what if we actually change the supply and demand curves and we bring back a lot more? Like what does the future look like? This has happened before, it happened in the 1800s with a metal called aluminum. Guys all know about this, right? Like we found a new process to make aluminum, all of a sudden it went from like the most baller shit you could have to we make airplanes out of it, it enables modern air travel.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: Some reason they now make the iPhone out of it, a terrible decision, but a different conversation. I hope there is a future like that that can be created with what we're doing by journeying out and unlocking the supply of platinum.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: And that rhodium is incredibly expensive, yeah? Like tens of thousands per ounce.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: Rhodium is very, very expensive. Iridium is incredibly expensive. But I think it's really important to have maybe a little bit more nuance view on metals here. The price that you sell an a gram for, an ounce for, however you want to quantify it, isn't necessarily the best marker of what the use case could be from a business standpoint. You really got to pay attention to market cap. And I think this is where a lot of people in mining get this wrong. The largest market cap in the world is gold. The second largest market cap in the world is the platinum group metals. There has to be a huge supply here on earth that we can go into. Because the reality is, the question we get asked all the time, which is very true, if we go find some new material on asteroid and bring it back, we destroy the market for that material. Right? There has to be a huge amount that can be there before we destroy the market. And with platinum group metals, there is. To give you guys a sense, it would take us about 100 missions before we would screw up the price of platinum.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Got it. So it's going to wind up being price competitive for you to collect this stuff if you succeed. And if you were to have like outlier success, you could actually change the market cap and how we look at these things, yeah?

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: I mean, J-Cal, the best platinum group metal mine in the world in South Africa has 14% gross margins. I just told you a story we could have 90% gross margins. That changes the way of how we go mine, right? That opens up a whole new future that we can all go and enjoy where we have abundance. And I think isn't that what every startup founder should be aiming for?

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Absolutely. All right. Well, listen, we are going to be rooting for you. And what's the date again for the next one so we can put it on our calendar and have you back on afterwards and share your learnings, and hopefully, great success.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: So the next one is IM3, currently right now scheduled for November of this year.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: November of this year. So excited. Will it be Starship or it's going to be Falcon you said?

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: No, it's going to be a Falcon 9 that goes to the moon. This is a Falcon 9 flight.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yeah. So the Falcon 9s are incredibly consistent at this point. Amazing.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Amazing. Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yeah, they've got such an incredible track record now, hundreds of them. All right, so we will book you to come on in November and we will get the update on your amazing progress. I think this is like third time's going to be a charm.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: I hope so guys, we need it. I feel good about it. Incredible. I'm in. It's literally like we're living in a science fiction novel.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: It really... yeah. This one when I was researching it last night, you're reading it and you're like I can't believe they're already doing... this sounds so purely theoretical and that's what I assumed. I was like oh well they're talking about in 10 years maybe we can do this. It's like they're already running missions. It's happening.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Circle is the best place on the internet to start growing your online community. There, I said it. You know why? Because I tried to find a community platform for my Founder University and for my Angel University programs and we couldn't find one. And after we'd started digging and digging, we found the perfect solution, Circle.so. It has all the most amazing features, like you can put in a course, like you have a CRM, you can do email, you have chat rooms and message boards. It is amazing.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Whether you're selling a course or you're building a community based on a topic or maybe you're starting a book club, maybe you're a coach, maybe you have a business, and maybe you want to build a community for everybody to get together and learn and share information. It's all inside of Circle. How do I know this? My team did a bake off. Once we started finding these platforms, it was like a Frankensite, all these different pieces, nobody had put them all together until Circle, and Circle's going to put it together for you. To get started, go to circle.so/twist and get $1,000 off the Circle Plus plan. That's circle.so/twist.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: This is the underappreciated thing about technology and capitalism, right? We're in a bit of an existential debate here in the United States over capitalism, over wealth disparity, everything. The truth is, when you set up a system like the United States or we were talking about Towne subnets that are designed to be brutally capitalist, what happens?

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: You're basically in a competition to see who can lower the price to the point that more unlocks happen. So in this case, what Elon did with lowering the price to get to space allows this company, AstroForge, to then build and stand on his shoulders. Now, AstroForge makes this work, then you have to say, 'Well, what is the second order impact?' which is what I tried to get to in our quick interview here.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Okay, well maybe the sovereignty of the United States versus, I think most of these palladiums are in Russia, China, South Africa, etc.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: Well, right, yes. South Africa and Russia are right now the world's two largest suppliers of these platinum group metals. So yeah, it totally changes your thinking to be like, we've built an entire world and civilization based on rhodium and iridium being very rare and you've got to go dig down in places you don't want to go to go get it. And what would change if all of a sudden it was like, 'No, we can go out to space, grab a rock, laser it up, and just fly the iridium right back to Earth.'

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: And so this is something important for all founders listening. You know, the better, cheaper, faster is kind of how we used to look at technology in the Wired 1.0 days, not the woke Wired magazine now that's anti-tech or like holding truth to power or whatever.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: The New York Times says it's not woke enough, Jace, just so you know. The New York Times says they're not even woke enough.

highlights: AstroForge is developing specialized spacecraft to mine platinum group metals from near-Earth asteroids using laser-directed energy for extraction and magnetic systems for landing. The company aims to make space mining economically viable by targeting $10 million mission costs for a potential $100 million return in rare commodities.

The Templar project on the Bittensor network successfully trained a 72-billion-parameter AI model using a decentralized network of distributed GPUs, proving that high-level machine learning can be achieved outside of centralized labs. This model incentivizes independent miners to contribute compute power, aiming to create "sovereign AI" that is accessible to nations and organizations with limited hardware resources.

Open Oats is an open-source, privacy-centric note-taking agent that utilizes local large language models to provide real-time fact-checking and insights during meetings without compromising data security. The project’s development illustrates a new era of software maintenance where AI "auto-maintainers" can autonomously manage GitHub repositories by fixing bugs and merging pull requests.

The massive $100 million-per-episode budget for the upcoming Harry Potter TV series represents a strategic shift toward high-fidelity world-building designed to maximize the lifetime value of "super fans." This economic model focuses on driving long-term streaming subscriptions and supporting secondary revenue streams like theme parks and global merchandise.
summary:
This episode of *This Week in Startups* explores the frontiers of deep tech, focusing on asteroid mining, decentralized artificial intelligence, and open-source productivity tools. Matt Gialich, CEO of AstroForge, discusses his company's mission to harvest platinum group metals (PGMs) from near-Earth asteroids, explaining how magnetic docking and laser-sorting technology could make space mining more economically viable and environmentally friendly than terrestrial alternatives. Shifting to AI, Sam Dare of Templar explains how BitTensor’s incentive-based network allowed his team to train a 72-billion-parameter model using decentralized compute for a fraction of the traditional cost. Both segments underscore a broader theme of using innovative infrastructure to disrupt high-capital industries.

The second half of the transcript highlights the democratization of software through AI and the economics of modern entertainment. Yassine Ali Rahim demos "Open Oats," a local-first, open-source AI note-taker that provides real-time fact-checking and due diligence during meetings. The hosts reflect on how tools like Claude enable solo developers to build and maintain complex software, potentially threatening established SaaS companies. Finally, the "Off Duty" segment covers the massive financial stakes of the streaming wars—specifically HBO’s $5 billion commitment to a Harry Potter series—and the expansion of the *Lord of the Rings* film franchise. The episode concludes with recommendations for AI dictation hardware and high-fidelity media, illustrating the hosts' ongoing obsession with the intersection of performance technology and pop culture.
